CURRERT AFFAIRS,

Cork’s “ Only National,”” the Ezaminer, is a pretty
paper, truly. Mr. Crosbie, the owner, or one of the
owners of the sheet, is President and Chairman of the
Cork Industrial Association, and that fact only adds
salt to a little piece of quite recent history. The cycle
firm of O’Callaghan, 73 Grand Parade, Cork, sent an
advertisement to Mr. Crosbie’s “ Only National.”” The
advertisement was headed :—“ A Common Complaint
and its Remedy. Don’t send money for strange bicycles,
on strange terms, to strange firms.”” The first two para-
graphs of the advertisement ran as follows:—“ When

 you see a very attractive advertisement, offering to send
2 machine to you at some ridiculous price on receipt of
very small sum down, reason the matter out, and
think of what you are about to do. During the past
fortnight three complaints have been made to us by
cople who sent their money and got no bicycle, though
heir money was kopt all right.” The advertisement

anyway was refused by the proprietors of the Cork
Feamener, who gave as the reason for refusal that some
of their Anglish advertisers might object to it. Mr.
Crosbie, let us remind our readers, is a high honorary
official of the Cork IZrish Industrial Association.
Messrs. O’Callaghan have issued a poster which as we
write is spread out before us on the floor of our office.
It is headed “ How.the Fzaminer helps Cork traders,”’
and then follows this announcement :—* The following
advertisement has heen refused by the proprietors of
the Cork Bazaminer, who state that some of their Eng-
lish advertisers might object to it as applying to them.””
The refused advertisement, from which we have quoted
two paragraphs, is then set out in full on the poster.
Naturally the poster has created quite a sensation in
Cork. Messrs. O’Callaghan have also issued a circular,
thousands of which have been distributed. On
that circular Messrs. O’Callaghan announce—"In
future no advertisement from us will appear in any
paper issued at the Hzaminer office.” We await fur-
ther developments and explanation from Mr. Crosbie of
the Cork Industrial Association.

At the half-yearly meeting of the Donegal Railway
Co., the Chairman, Mr. John A. Pomeroy, J.P., speak-
ing of the opposition to the Great Northern Bill, re-
marked :—“ Of course, neither the Midland, the Done-
gal Railway Company, nor the county had any in-
terest in that controversy, and it was exceedingly hard
that, in consequence of a difference of opinion with
which they had nothing to do, the shareholders of that
Company and the Donegal ratepayers affected should
lose anything, and the country at large should suffer by
the -indefinite postponement of railway and hotel de-
velopment.””  Would it not have been better if Mr.
Pomeroy had counselled the G. N. Railway Board to
accept the competitive examination instruction and go
ahead with the Bill? Does not the principle of com-
pefitive examination concern the Donegal Papists?
Amongst those present at the meeting was the first
“Idolator >’ in the county, Mr. James Dunlevy, Chair-
man of the Donegal County Council, and he apparently
made no protest when Mr. Pomeroy stated that the
Donegal Railway had no interest in the controversy.
Is Mr. Dunlevy a “tame,” and was he afraid to say
boo, for surely he must know that “ Idolators’’ have a
very material interest in the question of competitive
examination ?

We are not aware, but perhaps Mr. Dunlevy knows
better, that the imported Scotch Secretary gives entire
satisfacion in the matter of appointments and promo-
tions. The manager of tho Donegal Railway is an
Englishman who, we believe, has a reputation for fair
dealing. But since the Midland Railway arranged to
buy the line, the Secretary has more to do with appoint-
ments than he previously had. A vacancy occurred at
Castlefin station recently, and an “ Idolatrous ’’ station-
master at a smaller station was notified that he was to
be tramsferred there, but in due course he got instruc-
tions that he was to bo sent to a less important station.
A “Saved ’’ official of short experience has since got
the job.

An illuminated address, a gold medal and a purse of
sovereigns was recently presented to a great man down
South. Needless to say the intelligent foreigner who
had heard of such a presentation would ask what great
work had the recipient done to thus place the public
undor such an obligation to him. Had he created an
industry round which a thriving town had sprung up?
Iad he inspired the people to great natiomal en-
deavours? Had he written a great book or designed a

3b4

great architectural work? The intelligent foreigner
who awould ask such ‘questions wounld be a fish out of
water in this funny country. The recipient of the
illuminated address, the gold medal and the purse of
govereigns was a common bobby. The gifts were
‘presented by the patriotic peoplo of Kilmacow in the
county of Kilkenny, and the presentations were mado in
the Slieverue R.I.C. Barracks. Mr. Grant, J.P., was
in the chair, and amongst those present were
Messrs. Aylward, TFarvell, Williams, Saunders,
‘Dunphy, ‘Kelly, Higgins, Doody, Power and McCarthy.
‘The Chairman felt it an honour to preside on “an aus-
picious occasion of this kind.”’ The honorary treasurer
referred to “the courtesy, efficiency, and public spirit’’
displayed by policeman Collins “ during the time he had
charge of Kilmacow district.”” The treasurer also said
‘that the peeler’s “fine grasp of the progress of the
times was much admired.”

The address is coached in the usual plamdp and ex-
travagant terms. If some of theso addresses were in-
cluded in a work of fiction, most readers would probably
‘think ‘that they were overdrawn:—“ We, the under-
signed inhabitants of Kilmacow, etc.”” In the first two
:sentences we find the following words and phrases:—
“straightforward, zealous and efficient;”” * during
your career as sergeant in charge;’’ “tact, zeal and
integrity ;>’ “your cheerful temperament, manly, up-
right and honourable virtues;’’ “invariably chivalrous
spirit;”’ “hosts of admirers;’’ “every grade of the
community.”’ When we can pick that lot from the
first two sentences our readers can guess the rest. The

" last -paragraph of the address commences thus:—“In
leaving us to assume fresh responsibilities in a new fie'd
of labour, etc.”’ The italics are ours.

Needless to say the policeman could “hardly find
words to fully convey ’ his gratitude.  The latter,”
said the policeman, referring to the purse of sovereigns,
“is likely to vanish.”* We should say that it has al-
ready vanished in “treats.”” He stated that he was
“an ardent disciple of Isaac Walton.”” But what about
the concertina and the hair brush, for, as our readers
know, fishing for trout is only part of an Trish police-
man’s “labour;’’ playing concertinas and creasing their
hair represent other portions of the fields of labour ™
of the police force in this country.

A large lump of the shareholders’ money of the Great
Sick and Sourface Railway was consumed on Saturday
last in special trains and gorgeous luncheons.  Iis
Excellence the Lord High Humbugger, of the British
“Liberal >’ Government, declared the Rosslare route
open, and Mr. F. Keonigs, at whose refreshment rooms
it is, or was, impossible to purchase an Irish-madc
cigarette, was caterer. It was a ““ God-Save-the-King "’
function. The Grand Steward of the pernicious secret
society, the Freemasons in Ireland, presided, and pro-
posed ““the King,”’ and hoped he would visit Ireland
again where he ‘would bo “ most heartily welcomed by
the most warm-hearted people in ‘his Majesty’s wide
dominions.” The Freeman devoted three and a half
columns ‘to the ““God-Save-tho-King’’ proceedings.
Brother Goulding also proposed the toast of the Lord
Lieutenant, and the toast was drunk to the tune of the
British public-house lyric—‘ For He's a Jolly Good
Fellow.” The Marquis of Waterford and Lord Barry-
more, better known as Smith Barry, also spoke;
Brother Goulding presented Lord Aberdeen with a
silver cup, and Aberdoen expressed his thanks. The
“truly national ”’ Freeman tells us that “The caterer
for the luncheon was Mr. F. Keonigs, the contractor for
the railway refreshment rooms. It was served in
splendid style.”” And the poor sharcholders pay for
it all.

By the way, the Freeman is strangely silent about
the “ International Exhibition.”” Has Algernon Locker
frightened Mr. Brayden and Mr. Matty Bodkin?

There is an Anglo-Irish “ poem ” in another part of
this paper, edited by the President of the U.LL. of
We quote it in full :—

“Share,” sez Oi to meself, wid a will,

“Dere’s some generous folks left st:ll.”

Perhaps the President of the U.I.L. of Great Britain
now speaks with as “ grand ’’ an accent as the London
bobbies of whom he once wrote. Ho may have so com-
pletely forgotten English as she is spoken in Ireland
that he thinks we barbarians over ‘here say “ Oi,”
“arter,”” and “ Hinsolence!”

In a week or so Pilve na Maige, “ The Maigue
Poets,”” will make its appearance. It is an edition by
Father Dinneen of the works of the poets who lived in
the barony of Coshma, on the banks of the Maigue, in
the County of Limerick, in the 18th century. The
chief of these poets are O’Tuomy and M‘Grath. The
book promises to be the most interesting volume of Irish
poetry yet given to the public, and abounds in matter
of social, literary, and historical interest. Wo under-
stand that the editor has taken great trouble to make
the volume enjoyable by the ordinary student. Even
Mr. Bung will take a keen interest in the book, as
O’Tuomy belonged to the renowned fraternity of beer-
sellers. The book will be published by M. H. Gill and
Son, Ltd.

Just at this season when the question—“Where shall
we send our boys and girls for their education?’—is
troubling the minds of thousands of Irish parents, the
words of the Bishop of Cloyne, in the course of his
remarks at the distribution of prizes at the Loretto
Convent, Youghal, are very pertinent. His Lordship
said that : —

A great movement was extending itself over Ireland,
and a splendid cffort made to cncourage native industries, but
thero were those who laid it down as a condition of permanent
success that tho home-manufactured articles should equal in quality
and be as cheap as the foreign product. Well, there was onc
home industry in regard to which this condition was strictly
observed.  He roforred to the teaching supplied in their homo
Boarding Schools. He believed that a better education—and he
used the word education in its widest and most comprehensive
senso—was givan in their Irish boarding schools, whether for girls
or boys, and at far lower cost than in similar educational institu-
tions in England. Ho mentioned the matter with ‘the view of
influencing them to try to stop the unnccessary sending of children,
boys or girls, out of Trcland for their oducation.

The deplorable accident during a hurling match at
Drom, Templemore, on Sunday last that resulted in the
death of Mr. Michael Maher will raise in the minds of
many persons reflections on the dangers of hurling.
Whilst a player was drawing at the ball in the air, he
accidentally struck young Mr. Maher on the forehead
with his caman, felling him to the ground. Incidents
like this are not likely to enhance the popularity of
hurling in many quarters; and the game, like everything
else, must live on its merits, and stand the tests of fit-
ness and competition. The dangers of the game
are often, we believe, put forward as excuses for its not
being played in certain places. Cannot these dangers
be mitigated? Hurling is a sort of hockey, or rather
hockey is a sort of hurling; except in some details the
games, to an on-looker, are very much alike. ~We
believe that in hockey the stick must not be lifted
above the head.  Could.not that rule be introduced
into hurling without materially affecting the game as a
game?

A post-card, one side of which is a pictorial advertise-
ment of British cocoa and chocolate, is scarcely an ideal
medium for an official communication between the Sec-
retary of a Coiroe ;Ceanncaip and one of its members.
We have before us such a post-card from the Secretary
of the Coipoe Ceanncary, Cahirciveen.

The Forty-Foot Hole, Sandycove, is the last place in
the world that one could expect to be the sceno of
public meetings. The Forty-Foot Hole provides, if we
may put it that way, a wide platform. We remember
last year, as we were dressing ourselves after a swim,
listening to two old sinners, who were drying them-
selves as they discussed appreciatingly the views of
Mike McCarthy “the Catholic.”” We have been in
the water at the same time as men who would not be
sorry if we went to the bottom and did not come up
again. There is no distinction of race, class, creed,
politics or anything else at the Forty-Foot Hole: tho

There have heen meetings and speechifyings at the
Forty-Foot Hole. The Urban Council made a rule
that all persons should wear costumes after nine-o’clock.
For our own part we not only think this very reason-
able, but wo would approve their action if the rule
was applicable at all hours. A well-known frequenter
of the bathing-place, a gentleman who has for years
taken a great interest in it, was prosecuted and fined
for not complying with the regulations. — Certainly fhe
authorities might have hit on someone else to make an
example of, but that ‘is only a detail. —There were
suggestions ‘that the Urban Council wanted ‘to .grab
the Forty-Foot Hole, and run it at a profit, notwith-
standing that the placo has been kept up by woluntary .
subscriptions, and that a great deal of money has been
spent on improving the place. The Bathing :Committee
has been styled a “so-called Committee.”” We know
nothing of the Committee, or of how it :is
appointed, but it must be a difficult matter to
elect such a Committee, as the great mass of the
bathers are not likaly to take any interest in it. They
enjoy their swims and trouble no more about the mat-
ter, beyond, if they are decent people, subscribing a
few shillings voluntarily for the upkeep of the place.
The Chairman of the Urban Council declared that as far
as the knew the Council had no intention of seizing the
place. If people do not bring their own‘bathing apparel—
and men’s'bathing apparél can be carried ‘about as con-
veniently as a handkerchief—we ;presume ‘it ‘is ‘free to
any ‘private person to set'up a stall and lend ‘them cut
at a'price. If the Urban Council take on ‘that work, ‘in
what way is the rights of the public invaded? The
storm in the Forty-Foot Hole strikes us as something of
a storm in a ‘tea-cup; as the bathers’ public spirit is
now in a state of aectivity, perhaps it would smooth
matters if a public meeting were called to elect a
new .Committee .of ‘their choice.

Recently we published -a lebter concerning the need
of ‘suitable “readers’’ for wuse in :the ‘“National”’
Schools. The matter is practically in the hands of the
managers, and as at least, every Catholic manager is
probably a reader of the LEADER, we would ‘like to
draw their attention to “The Irish Temperance
Reader,”” by Father J. Halpin, P.P., of Scariff, Co.
Clare. This volume is published by Browne and Nolan,
and is very well printed and turned out. The book
has ‘already met with a considerable amount «of com-
mendation. Tf is admirably adapted for:school reading,
for at the end of every one of the seventeen chapters
there are a series of questions for examination \calcu-
lated to test the knowledge .of the pupil. The book is,
we understand, sanotioned by the ¢ National ’’ Board.

. “An Tnish Irelander’’ writes:— Dear Sir,—Look-
ing over my LEADER of this week, btwo matters have
struck me which I think worth fixing public attention
on, as it can be fixed only through the Lraper. The
fixst is the wisdom or patriotism of Irish manufacturers
giving a monopoly to one trader in a town. In one of
the most thriving towns in Treland I had occasion lately
to visit some ironmongers’ shops. With an Irish-Tre-
land eye I inspected their stock of bicycles, and found
them all English make. Asking why they had no Ivish-
made bicycles—Pierce’s for instance—I was told that
Pierce would not give his bicycles to a ny but one trader
in the town. T can’t understand what Pierce gains by
shutting out the sale of his bicycles from respectable
traders who do a considerable trade in that
line. Perhaps you can get an explanation

* from Messrs.  Pierce. The other matter is:
Irish names on carts. Why is this matter
left to a few individuals who can easily be dealt with,
or even to the Dublin Corporation? Why does not the
Gaelic League exhort every genuine member of it to
put his name in Irish on his cart, if he have a cart. I
believe if they did so our paternal Government would
soon see the utter absurdity of trying to keep out the
tide by petty prosecutions such as we have witnessed.
But Gaelic Leaguers seem not yet to have learned to
unite and fight together. My hope is in the influence
of the LEADER, hence my troubling you.”’

Scholarships and prizes are very much current’ topics
just now. We have not succeeded in obtaining a list
of the “Idolatrous’’ youths of the Nutting brigade.
The recent move of the Queen’s College, with which
“Imaal *’ deals in another column, lends an emphasis
to the scholarships which are freely given in various
Catholic Colleges. A reference to our advertising
columns this week will show what St. Colman’s, Fer-
moy, is doing in this direction. St. Colman’s offers
no less than nineteen scholarships of an aggregate
value of £255, and we refer our readers who have sons
capable of competing for them to a careful perusal of
the particulars.  Rockwell Collego, Cashel, also an-
nounces in our advertising columns eighteen scholar-
ships of £15 each, nine of which will be confined to
present students of the College, and nine to freshmen.

Whilst on the subject of scholarships, we would also
like to draw attention to some now offered by the De-
partment of Agriculture and Technical Instruction. By
their announcement published in another column, it
will be seen that the Department will grant a number
of commercial scholarships for the session 1906-7, of
the value of £100 each. The Department also propose
to establish two industrial scholarships of £80 cach, for
persons engaged in the woollen and leather industries.

HUMPHREY’S DUMP.

P UMPHREY’S Dumpery got a great fall, and

now all the King’s hangers-on, the Trolley-pcle
men and the railway nation-killers are frantically en-
deavouring to lift Humphrey’s Dumpery up again.
They have their work cut out for them. The whole
business from its inception has been an imposition. At
the meeting some years ago the promoters had recourse
to outraging the public by calling in the police. The
people do not want Humphrey’s Dump; a few who
stand to gain are anxious for it; its organ is M.
William Murphy’s ha’penny Zndependent, and Mr.
Murphy’s Independent publishes a half-column of Irish
every day by way of sop to Gaelic Leaguers, whilst the
Gaelic League is formally and deliberately committed
against Humphrey’s Dump. The United Irish League
in convention assembled condemned the Imposition also,
the Freeman’s Jowrnal has taken sides vigorously
against it.

There was a good deal of whistling by way of keeping
up their courage at the semi-private meeting held last
week to boom the Imposition. However, it is a bad
look out for the guarantors, but we cannot offer them
any sympathy, and when they are paying up their share
after the failure is over we will find it difficult to re-
frain from saying “ we told you so.”’

The attendance and the speeches at the boom of
Humphrey’s Imposition are instructive. The Marquis
of . Ormonde—what has he ever done in addition to
“living on his means ’’ *—proposed “ The King.”” Mr.
Justice Ross proposed ‘ Humphrey’s Dump,’’ and Mr.
William Murphy of the Independent, with its half-
column of Irish, responded. Mr. William Murphy de-
clared, “ we had no object in view but the good of the
country.”” It would display a certain crudeness per-
haps to retort directly to that declaration. We are
willing to believe that Mr. Murphy can so manipulate
his mind that the swelling of the Tramway receipts and
the receipts of other ventures with which he is asso-

ciated, equate  with the good of the country ’’ in his
strictly business way of looking at things.
Justico Ross talked of “a broad tolerance.” Well,

Iumphrey’s Dump people did not apparently show
much broad tolerance in their press invitations, for it
would seem that they excluded that press that is con-
vinced that the project is a national evil. Apparently
the Kreeman got no invitation to inspect the Imposition
in the making. We got no invitation. Had we got
an invitation we might, or might not, have looked
round the Dump with critical eyes, though certainly
wo should not have gone to the “ God-Save-the-King *’
luncheon. Perhaps some of the speakers’ protested a
Alderman Irwin, a public gentleman
whom it is difficult to catalogue any more definitely than
as “a smart fellow,”” said that Humphrey’s Dump was
not “for the benefit of any individuals, nor for the
reward of any syndicate, but for the good of the
country at large.””  Well, we can all believe as much of

ﬂfl&t as we like, and for our part we don’t believe any
of it.

Let us glance at a few of the names of those who
were present—of course the Tramway Company, which
may for short be called the Promoter, was well repre-
sented. Napoleon Dent of the Great Sourface Railway,
and Algernon Locker of the Ziish Z'mes, the libeller ot
Canon McInerny, were present. We only read tho
name of one Catholic Clergyman amongst the list given
in_ Alf Fowx, and that one was the inevitable Dublin
Castle diner-out, Monsignor Molloy; we are sure he
preferved. lunching, in Humphrey-Dump company than
cven talking eloquently about hedge schools. Sir John
Nutting of: the “ Trinity ** bribes was present also. 1o
ought to exhibit a choice selection of his Papist
scholars; we would certainly go to the Imposition to
see them; and by way of distinguishing them he could
specially mark each of them with a black mark on the
leg. There were only ten men with “ O’ before their
names at the Dump boom, and amongst those we were
sorry. to see the name of Mr. P. J. O’Neill, Chairman
of the Dublin County Council. Almost needless to say
Tony Traill, of the famous Spelling Bee, graced the
occasion with his presence.  Taken as a whole, the
gathering was thoroughly unrepresentative of Ireland,
even though: here and there we read an odd name whose
owner ought to have known better.

The enterprise has been persisted in in face of the
voice of the nation. Are Mr. William Murphy and
his Zndependent, with its sop to Irish Ireland in the
shape of half-a-column of Irish, fo be permitted to
defy the voice of the Irish people? Listen to M.
Algernon Locker, an importation on the Zrish 7'imes.
According to Mr. Murphy’s Zndependent, Mr. Locker,
in reply to ““The Press,”’ said:—There had been
some opposition on the part of a smalk section of the
Press, but that had now almost vanished. Indeed the
idea of an Exhibition was considered so good even by
that section. thiat they thought they shou!d have two,
not one.”” That is particularly an attempted blow be-
tween the eyes at the Deplorable. Now, Matty, it is
your: turn next, or do you purpose taking it lying down
from Algernon?

The question that must be faced boldly is—what is
to be done with reference to Humphrey’s Tmposition ?
The West Britons are committed to attempt to carry
on this Imposition in face of the opposition of the
Gaelic League, the United Irish League and National
opinion generally. ~The Dumpers from England and
Scotland, and the Jew Hawkers and huxtering Turks
will come along seeking to increase the already nation-
killing and emigration producing hold that foreign
goods have on the Irish market. We see by Mr.
Shanks’s statement that amongst the British exhibitors
will be an English Candle Company and an English
Match Company. Doubtless the Industrial Revival has
hit the Irish market for foreign candles and matches
pretty severely, and the foreigners are anxious to avail
of the chance offered them to regain some of their lost
ground by means of Humphrey’s Dump. Are the Irish
manufacturers to be intimidated into exhibiting at the
Dump? That is a great and pressing question. The
Irish manufacturers do not want the Imposition, bub
finding that foreign competitors are taking advantage of

the jumping-off board provided them by Mr. William
Murphy and his friends, they may feel inclined to suc-
cumb to the temptation to go in in self-defence. Tt is
most desirable that the Trish manufacurers should have
the accredited voice of the people ab their backs in a
policy of declining to be intimidated into this Imposition
on the people of Treland by a collection of toadics,
‘West Britons, and interested persons. Wo are quite
satisfied' that the unfortunate Irish manufacturers do
not want this Humphrey’s Dump. Now, a newspaper
support, though powerful in its way, is not sufficient to
meet the case. The manufacturers, if possible, ought
to take combined action themselves, and agree to ab-
stain from Humphrey’s Dump. The various national
and: Irish bodies that in any way are concerned with
Irish industrial progress should likewise move. Do

VERYBODY knows that the Queen’s College,
Belfast, is mainly a Presbyterian institution,
almost a Presbyterian close borough. Perhaps every-
one does not know equally well how it became so.  If
not, they can make themselves fully acquainted with
the matter by studying the important “ Letter * on the
Irish University Question, published some two years ago
by the President of the University College, Dublin.
When the Queen’s Colleges were about to be established,
some of the leaders of the Presbyterian body in Ulster pub
themselves in communication with the Ministry of the
day. Sir Robert Peel was then Premier, and an
eminent Northern lay Presbyterian wrote him a letter
intimating that the presence of a single Roman Catholic
on the staff of the Arts Faculty would prevent the Pres-
byterians of Ulster from using the proposed College at
Belfast. The explanation of this, of course, was that
the Presbyterians wished to use the new College for the
Arts course of their divinity students. The ultimatum
succeeded, for there has never been a Catholic on the
staff of the Arts Faculty in the Queen’s College, Bel-
fast, and the Presbyterians have not only condescended
to use the College, but have almost made their own
of it.  Some years ago the occupiers of the close
borough applied to the Government for an additional
endowment for their College.  This modest request:
drew forth Dr. Delany’s “ Letter *’ already veferred to,
protesting against the granting of any such application
unless the whole Irish University Question were dealt
with, and the claims of University College recognised.
Ile showed by statistics that the St. Stephen’s Green
College was doing more and better work in the Arts
courso of the Royal University than all the Queen’s
Colleges put together, and that to give any further
endowment to even the most successful of tho Queen’s
Colleges, without putting a College which was doing
far more work upon an equal footing as regards funds,
would have been simply iniquitous, not merely to say
inequitable.  Well, the “TLetter ”’ attained its end ;

the job was not done, and the canny gentlemen of
North-east Ulster had to grind their teeth over another
triumph of “denominationalism’’—that is, of “ Roman’’
denominationalism, for the triumph of the Presbyterian

From the very beginning of owr movement I have
insisted strenuously on the fact that, if we wish to look
for real Ivich we must lock for it in the mouths of
living speakers of Irish. For a long time the idea was
looked upon by many persons as too absurd to be en-
tertained. They said: “ What! you are contradicting
all human experience. If a person wishes to look for
good English is he to go to look for it in the mouth of
the uneducated Cockney or Yorkshireman$'’

In the face of that sort of talk it is refreshing to
find a man like Dr. Shechan “always approaching the
speech of the people with a spirit of reverence.”” If
the English people had been trampled into the very
eanth since the days of Chaucer, all their books burned
and their writers murdered, where should a person go
to-day to look for the English language? He certainly
would not find much of it in libranies. I do not think he
would find much of it in Cockneydom nor in York-
shire. The Irish which is now living in the mouths of
our people, after all the crushing and trampling and
burning, is a real, well-ordered, -classical human
speech, standing on exactly the same fundamental prin-
ciples on which it stood a thousand years ago. Any
person who will study that fact to-day, as Dr. Sheehan
has been studying it for some time, will not fail to see
in it something very like a language miracle. Then he
will do as Dr. Sheehan has done, he will “ approach it
with reverence.”’ He will “refrain from touching the
winged word with desecrating hand.””

‘What vandals some of our scholars have been in this
matter! What precious gems they have flung away
as rubbish !

I acknowledge most heartily our obligations to Dr.
O’Donovan, who cdited the Four Masters, but it is
saddening to read some of the notes in which he “ cor-
rects *’ the language of the Annals for us, and then to
find that the Annals ave right and that the “correction’”
is wrong.

Mapban says he is looking forward to critical com-
ments from me on Dr. Sheehan’s book. I would have
given those comments before now but for a resolution
which I have recently come to. It is full time that I
should stand aside and let others do the criticising of
Irish hooks according as they appear.  There are
several others now who are well able to do the criticis-
ing  Mapvdin himself, for example, is one. If I
criticise a book I must speak of its good qualities and
say nothing of its faults. That is not fair either to
tho public or to the language. If T criticise a book and
point out even ono fault in ib, the public, not being
themselves judges as yet, will jump to the conclusion
that the book is no good at all. Then the sale of the
book is injured, the writer is prevented from getbing
the reward which he has well earned by his arduous
work din an unpromising ficld.

It is in overy way better that I “should not take
a hand,”” and in future I shall not. T shall let writers
and critics play out the “ game’’ between themselves.
I am quite sure that, by doing that, I shall learn a
great deal which T could not learn if T were interfering
in the “game.” 5

THE EYOLUTION OF IRISH ART.

NLESS we are a nation we are nothing, and

the growth of a civilisation springing from
tho Toots of one of the oldest in Europe will alone make
us a nation, give us scope to grow naturally, give us
somebhing to inspire what is best in us, cultivate our
national pride and self-respect, and encourage our self-
dependence.”’ 2 e e

What on earth has all this quotation from the “ Philo-
sophy of Inish Ireland ”? to do with Art? Very much—
everything, in fact, as I hope to show.

It would appear that what is really needed is not so
much to reform Art in Ireland as to Irishise it. We
surely do not wish to oust German art merely to replace
it by paintings of the English School. Or even of the
Italian or Flemish. No, we should desire an Irish
School of Painting. How, then, are we to obtain such?
By “ giving it scope to grow naturally.”

Had our country been allowed to retain her own
civilisation, we should undoubtedly by this time have
painters as great and pictures as beautiful as any in
Burope; our Art, like our language and our customs,
has suffered from the Anglicisation forced on it. And
after our young LEApER had come to the aid of the Gaelic
League, and forcibly wrenched the mind of Ireland from
the foreign soil which was rotting its very roots away,
we, with the new pride and strength of Irish manhood
which it brought to us, coursing in our veins, had yet
to go back humbly and learn owr native speech as a
littlo child.

Let us do the same with Art, beginning humbly, and
letting it spring from the roots, being inspired by, and
cvolved from, “ what is best in us.””

What, after all, is the true end and aim of Art? Is
it that the Artist may earn the praise of the Critics?
Or cven that he should rise to fame and glory as a
great painter? No, Art has a greater and more noble
end. It is to bring the True and the Beautiful into
the lives of the common people; to raise them by its
contemplation above the lowness and sordidness of lile,
and teach them to know and love the eternal beauty of
the Creator. And the Artist and the Poet are alike in
this, that the power to comprehend the Creator’s plea-
sure in His creation has boen given them in greater
measure, and with it, the power also to reproduce for
the benefit of their less-favoured brethren, the harmony
and the beauty with which their own souls are flooded.
And by the term Poet, I do not only mean the masters
of verse, but include also all writers .whose work, like
that of Lytton and the author of the book quoted above,
1nspires noble thoughts and great deeds, and raises the
mind above the daily fret and littleness of mortal life.

One day recently I was shown a small picture—
merely an artistic colour-print, but which had evidently
beon designed by one who had an artist’s perception. It
was merely a section of a pond or brook, with white
waber-lilies and broad green leaves floating on its sur-

9

face; the pure freshness of the flowers and opening -

buds, and the slight violet hazo over a farther vista of
rushes, showed that the picture had been drawn in the
carly morning before the sun’s rays had touched them.
And, gazing on it, I was no longer in a dingy room in
the midst of machinery, but in the country by the pond
breathing in the morning air, and watching the little

splashes in the water where the frogs were leaping and - *

diving.
And when I laid tho picture aside and resumed my
work, it had refreshed me as a cup of cold spring water

on a sultry c!zmy 5 whilst for a considerable time after-
wards my mind was filled, not with life’s inevitablo -

worries and commonplaces, but with thoughts of the

leafy. retreats and their inhabitant fairvies,
Lytton in his “ Pilgrims of the Rhine.”’

The mission, then, of the Arbist is to raise and refine

- the minds of the people, not to hedge himself round
with the wsthetic, and talk scornfully of less-gifted
mortzls} And to those who, in Ircland Lu-(k\y,bhuve
the Arf’ls,t"s power of reproducing the beauty which they
perceive in their own minds, I say with all carnestness—-
give it expression! Whether it be merely the beauty
of a flower shown up unexpectedly, or the more ambi-
tiious picture of a landscape, or of the sea, or of human
life, let it take on visible form!

Fear not the critics, but paint the picture which is
before your inner consciousness, and thus bring home
to the minds of others the beauty which they might
not else have seen without your aid. Was it, think
you, the counsel or instruction of critics which inspired
such painters as Fra Angelico or Perugin? And be
not dismayed to find the picture on canvas so inferior
to the high ideal existing in your mind, for true beauty
is so spiritual and unearthly that no mere mortal can
hope to give material existence to all the beauty which
his own soul is capable of perceiving and loving. Yet
there should be no ugly realism permitted in your paint-
ing, but aim at always depicting the beautiful. What
good end would be gained by painting, for instance, the
picture of a drunken sot outside a tavern door? People,
cspecially the people in towns, see too much of the ugly
side of life already; they have no need of such in a
picture, whether for warning or amusement.

After all; whatever is true to nature is
ugliness is merely due to a defect, ugliness and defor-
mity having come into the world with sin, and being
caused by sin, which is a violation of the order and har-
mony of creation. But truth is always beautiful, for
Truth is God, and all created beauty is but a faint
reflection from His.

Tt may be objected that the picture of the drunkoun
sot is true in every detail? Granted; still the element
of truth is lacking somewhere, or the picture would not
bz ugly. The defect consists in this, that the subject
of the picture is not true; he has not been true to
nature, the high rational nature bestowed on man,
which he has degraded and denied, making it untrue in
his case. And painting him thus but serves to per-
petuate the untruth. The Artist and the deep thinker
will recognise the truth of my argument, although to
some it may seem obscure.

So let our Irish Art represent only what is best and
noblest. If we paint man, Igb us paint him as a sturdy
young Gael—strong in soul and body; or if as an old
man, one on whose visage the lines of goodness and
truth are clearly defined. If we paint scenery, let us
paint it in its beauty, not as a sordid street scene.

Satire is useful, and often necessary in writing, but
it should not be employed in painting, but left to the
caricaturist whose work mocks the follies of a day, and
is but intended to last for a day.
